comment.body:
you can instantly recognize two things: 
first is: when the tangent line is horizontal (paralell to x-axis), its value is zero... and when is it horizontal? - when the function that we derivate, has local min or max.
second is, that there is an inflexion point on the original function from were the rate of change starts to slow down, and the slope starts to go back towards zero. This is when the derivative has local max or min.
comment.raw_body:
you can instantly recognize two things: 
first is: when the tangent line is horizontal (paralell to x-axis), its value is zero... and when is it horizontal? - when the function that we derivate, has local min or max.
second is, that there is an inflexion point on the original function from were the rate of change starts to slow down, and the slope starts to go back towards zero. This is when the derivative has local max or min.

No.
it has nothing to do with that.
look at the gif charts I shared.
